title: From Betterment to Development: Discipling Youth as Community Builders
summary: This session is the culminating synthesis of the three-session Community Youth Development arc (Sessions 9–11). Having introduced the 40 Developmental Assets framework (Session 9) and the Asset-Based Community Development paradigm (Session

blocks.title: Overview
blocks.text:
This session is the culminating synthesis of the three-session Community Youth Development arc (Sessions 9–11). Having introduced the 40 Developmental Assets framework (Session 9) and the Asset-Based Community Development paradigm (Session 10), this session now weaves them together through the Community Youth Development (CYD) framework — and connects both to the theological task of youth discipleship. The central claim of CYD is stated powerfully in Karen Pittman's phrase: 'Community Youth Development helps us move from seeing young people as problems to seeing young people as problem-solvers and leaders. Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged.'

The CYD framework synthesizes two parallel claims: first, that every community contributes to the development of its young people, and every young person can offer gifts and experiences in the process of community development and transformation. This means that effective youth ministry is not a one-directional delivery of services to passive recipients but a partnership of mutual contribution. The session introduces the Developmental Pathway Model — a four-stage progression from Youth as Recipients (mobilizing resources to provide services for youth) through Youth as Resources (equipping youth to support others) through Youth as Assets (mobilizing youth to contribute to community betterment) to Youth as Leaders (developing youth who grow into adults equipped to transform their community). Most youth ministries, even excellent ones, operate primarily at the Recipients stage — this session challenges participants to envision and plan for the full developmental pathway.

The theology of youth discipleship introduced in Session 9 through Kenda Creasy Dean's Consequential Faith framework is deepened here through DVULI's four-goal model: equipping youth for Consequential Faith (creed, community, call, hope), Thriving in School, Work, and Life, and Community Engagement and Leadership. The Jackson case study — in which four DVULI participants (Rev. Leroy Burnett, Danny Agosto, Randall Jones, and Sandra Cestero) collaborate on a proposal for the Windsor Court Community Center — demonstrates CYD principles in action, and becomes the vehicle for a group strategic planning exercise. Participants leave this session with a specific 30-day action plan for shifting at least one aspect of their ministry practice from betterment toward development.
blocks.html:
Session 11

From Betterment to Development: Discipling Youth as Community Builders

The culminating synthesis of the Community Youth Development arc — weaving together Developmental Assets, Asset-Based Community Development, and a theology of whole-person discipleship

Imagine a youth pastor — call her Maria — who has led an excellent after-school tutoring program for six years. Every week, thirty neighborhood teenagers show up to receive homework help from volunteer tutors. Attendance is consistent. Parents are grateful. The church board is proud. By every traditional measure, it is a successful ministry. But Maria has begun to feel an uncomfortable weight. The students file in, receive help, file out, and return the following week in exactly the same posture: needing help, receiving help, leaving. The best students graduate and move on. The program continues as it always has. Something is missing, though Maria cannot yet name it.

What Maria is beginning to sense is the difference between betterment and development — the very distinction that Robert Lupton described as the line between programs that improve conditions for people and programs that build the capacity of communities to transform themselves from within. In this session, we name that difference clearly, we equip you to act on it, and we anchor the whole enterprise in the most compelling reason of all: this is precisely what it means to disciple young people as followers of Jesus Christ.

Completing the Arc: Where Sessions 9, 10, and 11 Connect

This session is the third and final movement in DVULI's Community Youth Development arc. In Session 9, you were introduced to the 40 Developmental Assets — the Search Institute's research-based framework identifying the internal and external building blocks that young people need to grow up healthy, resilient, and purposeful. You assessed three young people from your ministry context and discovered, likely with some sobering clarity, which assets were most and least present in their lives. You also encountered Kenda Creasy Dean's concept of Consequential Faith — the four characteristics that distinguish highly devoted young people from their peers who hold only a nominal or "moralistic therapeutic" form of Christianity (Dean, 2010).

In Session 10, you shifted focus from the individual to the neighborhood. Through John Kretzmann's Asset-Based Community Development (ABCD) framework, you learned to read your community not through its deficits but through its gifts — the skills of individual residents, the power of citizens' associations, and the resources of local institutions. You walked your community with your youth, photographed assets you had never noticed, and began to see the neighborhood differently: not as a problem to be solved, but as a place where God is already at work (Kretzmann, 1995).

Session 11 now asks: what happens when you put these two streams together? What emerges when positive youth development meets asset-based community development? The answer is Community Youth Development (CYD) — and its central claim is both simple and revolutionary.

Facilitator Note

Before presenting new content, spend five minutes in small groups reviewing one insight from each of Sessions 9 and 10. This retrieval practice activates prior learning and creates genuine "aha" moments as the synthesis becomes clear. Ask: "What surprised you most from the community asset walk? What stayed with you from the Developmental Assets assessment?"

The Central Claim: Every Community, Every Young Person

Community Youth Development, as articulated by Karen Pittman and the Forum for Youth Investment, rests on two parallel claims that must be held together. The first is that every community contributes to the development of its young people. Young people do not develop in isolation from the neighborhoods, congregations, schools, and relationships that surround them. Whether those environments are nurturing or harmful, they are never neutral. The 40 Developmental Assets framework gives us a vocabulary for naming what those contributions look like when they are working well. The ABCD framework gives us a methodology for strengthening those contributions by mobilizing what is already present.

The second claim is the one most youth ministries have not yet fully inhabited: every young person can offer gifts and experiences in the process of community development and transformation. This claim is not optimistic sentiment — it is a theological and empirical conviction. Theologically, it flows from the biblical truth that every person is created in the image of God and has been given gifts for the building up of the Body (1 Corinthians 12:7; 1 Peter 4:10-11). Empirically, it is confirmed in the story of Lateefah Simon, who moved from unenthusiastic participant in a youth development program to executive director — and then to MacArthur Foundation Fellow — because someone saw her gifts before she could see them herself (Raskin, 2005).

"Community Youth Development helps us move from seeing young people as problems to seeing young people as problem-solvers and leaders. Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged."

— Karen Pittman, Forum for Youth Investment

Read that phrase again slowly: Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged. This is not merely a motivational slogan. It is a complete critique of two common approaches to youth ministry. The first approach — betterment — aims to make young people problem-free by addressing their deficits. It is, at its best, compassionate and necessary. But it stops too soon. The second approach — a more sophisticated version of betterment — aims to prepare young people with skills, knowledge, and faith. This is better, but it still stops short of the full vision. The third approach — development in the fullest sense — engages young people as active contributors to community transformation. It is this third approach that CYD demands of us.

The Developmental Pathway Model: Four Stages of Youth Engagement

The DVULI curriculum presents a four-stage model for describing how youth can progressively move from passive recipients of ministry services toward becoming active leaders of community transformation. This Developmental Pathway Model is not a ladder of prestige — it is a map of growing participation and contribution. Most youth ministries, even excellent ones, operate primarily at Stage 1. The challenge of this session is to help you envision and plan for progression through all four stages.

Stage 1: Youth as Recipients

At this stage, the ministry mobilizes resources to provide services for youth. Programs are designed by adults, delivered by adults, and received by young people who are positioned as beneficiaries. This is not inherently wrong — it is where all ministry must begin. The tutoring program that brings students off dangerous streets and into a caring space is doing something real and important. The danger is when Stage 1 becomes the permanent ceiling of our ambition for young people, and when we unconsciously communicate that they have nothing to contribute beyond their presence as recipients of our care.

Stage 2: Youth as Resources

At this stage, the ministry equips and mobilizes youth to support others. The student who was once tutored becomes a tutor. The teenager who was mentored begins mentoring a younger child. The young person who participated in a drama program becomes a drama leader. Danny Agosto's story in the Jackson Times is a powerful illustration of this stage: Juan Mendoza, seeing Agosto's gift for connecting with younger children on the basketball court, gave him a job as a team coordinator — and in that moment, an at-risk gang member began the journey that would eventually lead him to a Master's degree in Theology and a Latino Leadership Award (Jackson Times, 2024).

Stage 3: Youth as Assets

At this stage, the ministry mobilizes youth to contribute to community betterment. Young people are no longer just helping within a ministry program — they are contributing to the wider community as agents of positive change. This is the stage at which the 40 Developmental Assets of individual young people begin to express themselves as community assets. A teenager with strong "service to others" assets does not just volunteer — she organizes. A young person with strong "positive identity" assets does not just participate in a neighborhood cleanup — he leads one. Stage 3 represents the organic connection between individual development and community development that CYD envisions.

Stage 4: Youth as Leaders

At this stage, the ministry is developing youth who grow into adults equipped to transform their community. This is not just leadership development within the ministry program — it is the cultivation of the next generation of community leaders who will carry forward a vision of community transformation long after they have aged out of any particular youth program. Lateefah Simon's trajectory — from petty thief to MacArthur Fellow to civic leader — represents Stage 4 in its fullest expression (Raskin, 2005). The DVULI Jackson cohort's Windsor Court proposal, which we will examine in detail, demonstrates what Stage 4 looks like when youth workers collaborate to create the conditions for it.

Think About It

Before moving forward, think about one specific program or activity in your ministry. Which stage of the Developmental Pathway best describes how youth currently participate in that program? What would it concretely look like for that program to shift to the next stage? Use the interactive widget below to explore this question with your cohort.

🗺️ Youth Developmental Pathway Mapper

Think of one specific program or ministry activity. Select the stage that currently best describes how youth participate in that program. Then explore what it would take to move up the pathway.

Upgrade Questions for Your Program
    Jackson Cohort Example

    A Theology of Youth Discipleship: Beyond Moralistic Therapeutic Deism

    Why does the shift from betterment to development matter theologically? The answer begins with an honest reckoning with the state of youth faith formation in American churches. Since 2001, sociologist Christian Smith and his colleagues at Notre Dame have been studying the spiritual lives of American adolescents — research summarized in landmark works including Soul Searching (2005) and Almost Christian (Dean, 2010). The findings are sobering for everyone in youth ministry.

    Smith found that while most American teenagers hold religious beliefs, those beliefs are surprisingly shallow and inarticulate. The dominant theology he identified — what he called Moralistic Therapeutic Deism — is characterized by a distant, undemanding God who is mainly concerned that everyone feel good and be nice to one another. One seventeen-year-old African-American Protestant girl summarized it this way: "I guess I'm a Christian, but I'm one of those still trying to figure everything out. I believe there's a higher power, but that's about all I know for sure" (Smith, 2005, as quoted in DVULI LW2 Workbook). This is not the faith of disciples who follow Jesus into the suffering of the world. It is, as Dean observed, the faith of a "moral therapeutic" self — and organized religion is largely producing it.

    What would a genuinely consequential faith look like? Drawing on Smith's data to identify what distinguished the most highly devoted teenagers from their peers, Dean (2010) described four consistent characteristics:

    • A creed to believe — the ability to articulate beliefs about a God who is both personal and powerful. These young people do not hold a vague theism; they have a vocabulary for what they believe and why.
    • A community to belong to — including meaningful relationships with adults who speak honestly with them about faith and life. Not the insulated peer-only gatherings so common in youth group culture, but intergenerational relationships of genuine accountability and care.
    • A call to live out — lives oriented by a sense of divine vocation to serve others, rather than the pursuit of self-satisfaction. These young people do not experience faith as a restraint on their ambitions; they experience it as a summons toward a purpose larger than themselves.
    • A hope to hold onto — a sense that their lives are part of a larger story that is going somewhere, guided by God. This hope is not naive optimism; it is the eschatological confidence that the arc of history bends toward the Kingdom.

    Now hold these four characteristics alongside the Developmental Pathway Model, and something remarkable comes into focus. A young person operating as a Recipient in your ministry is unlikely to develop a strong sense of call — because they have never been invited to contribute. A young person who has never been mobilized as a community Asset will struggle to maintain a robust hope — because they have never experienced the transformative joy of seeing their gifts change something in the world. The developmental pathway is not merely a framework for program design; it is a pathway toward consecrated personhood.

    DVULI's Three Goals of a Discipleship Ministry

    DVULI's approach to youth discipleship integrates the CYD framework with Dean's theology of consequential faith into three interdependent goals that together describe what whole-person ministry looks like. Understanding these three goals as a unified vision — not a checklist — is essential for ministry leaders who want to move beyond betterment toward genuine development.

    Goal 1: Consequential Faith

    The first goal is equipping young people for a faith that is consequential — not merely nominal. This means intentionally building all four of Dean's characteristics into every dimension of ministry: creed (teaching that takes doctrine seriously and gives youth a vocabulary for their beliefs), community (creating genuine intergenerational relationships, not just peer gatherings), call (inviting young people into meaningful service and leadership), and hope (narrating their lives within God's grand story of redemption and restoration). The prophetic warning in the DVULI curriculum is real: despite strong levels of spiritual activity during teen years, 61% of today's young adults disengage from active faith by their twenties (DVULI LW2 Workbook, citing Barna Research). Consequential faith is the antidote.

    Goal 2: Thriving in School, Work, and Life

    The second goal recognizes that discipleship that does not engage the whole person — including their academic and vocational development — is incomplete. Sandra Cestero's observation in the Jackson Times is telling: "Instead of focusing on spiritual values and character building, a lot of our programs revolve around tutoring and teaching kids basic skills like reading. Even those who are on track to graduate high school have difficulty with the basics of filling out a job application" (Jackson Times, 2024). Effective youth development ministry attends to the concrete realities of thriving in the world — not as a substitute for spiritual formation, but as an expression of it. The 40 Developmental Assets framework, especially its "commitment to learning" and "social competencies" categories, provides a concrete vocabulary for this goal.

    Goal 3: Community Engagement and Leadership

    The third goal is the one most neglected by traditional youth ministry, and the one most powerfully addressed by CYD. It is not enough to form young people who are spiritually mature and personally capable. The goal is to develop young people who are actively engaged in the transformation of the communities where they live. This is not an optional extension of discipleship for the especially motivated — it is the natural expression of what it means to follow Jesus who, as Ted Travis wrote, sees every young person not as a rock to be managed but as a cathedral to be built (Travis, 2015, as cited in DVULI LW2 Pre-Assignment). Community engagement and leadership is the full-fledged expression of Stage 4 in the Developmental Pathway.

    Facilitator Debrief Tip

    After presenting the three goals, invite participants to rate their current ministry on a simple 1–5 scale for each goal. Ask: "Where is your ministry strongest? Where is there the greatest gap?" This honest self-assessment creates the emotional urgency for the Stop, Start, Continue, Improve exercise that closes the session. Allow space for honesty — shame is not the goal, but clarity is.

    CYD in Action: The Windsor Court Case Study

    Abstract frameworks become transformative only when they are tested in concrete situations. The Jackson DVULI cohort's response to the Windsor Court Community Center controversy gives us exactly that opportunity. The Jackson Times reported that a developer, backed by three new upscale residents and city planning officials, proposed to convert the abandoned Windsor Court Community Center into office and retail space — Starbucks, a law firm, a frozen yogurt shop, and a farmers' insurance branch. For long-term residents of a neighborhood that was 43% African-American and 47% Latino, the writing on the wall was clear: gentrification was arriving, and the community's most valuable gathering space was about to become inaccessible to them (Jackson Times, 2024).

    Four DVULI participants decided they would respond — not with protest alone, but with a proposal. Understanding both the power of ABCD and the potential of CYD, they set out to imagine an alternative use for the space that would mobilize the community's youth as genuine partners in neighborhood transformation. Let's meet them again briefly:

    • Rev. Leroy Burnett (Senior Pastor, Leonard Avenue Community Church) — saw the Windsor Court situation as a way to reconnect his aging congregation with the neighborhood and to give the church's dwindling youth program something more exciting and purposeful than it had ever offered.
    • Danny Agosto (Youth Pastor, Iglesia Pentecostal Unida) — saw the proposal as a natural extension of his mentoring initiative. Community service and community leadership fit perfectly with his strategy of replacing the gang's false promises of belonging and purpose with genuine alternatives.
    • Randall Jones (Recreation Coordinator, Urban Center for Success) — had already developed arts and recreation programs that young people loved. He knew several graduates who were ready to become program leaders, working with him to develop the next generation of community youth leaders.
    • Sandra Cestero (Youth Leader and Program Coordinator, Grace Christian Family Center) — had run a successful tutoring and basic skills program for years and had begun to struggle with the recurring problem of her star graduates having nowhere to go next. She was hungry for a vehicle that would allow those graduates to stay involved as contributors rather than departing as alumni.

    What united these four leaders was precisely what CYD demands: each of them had already moved, in their own ministries, at least partway along the Developmental Pathway. Each of them was already thinking about youth not just as recipients of services but as resources with gifts. The Windsor Court proposal gave them an opportunity to imagine, together, what Stage 3 and Stage 4 could look like at a neighborhood scale. Use the simulation below to experience the decisions they faced and test your own CYD intuitions.

    🏙️ Jackson Case Study Decision Simulator

    You are part of the Jackson coalition. Work through five critical decisions about the Windsor Court proposal. After each choice, receive analysis showing how your decision aligns with ABCD, CYD, and the DVULI Core Values.

    Rev. Burnett · Church
    Danny Agosto · Mentoring
    Randall Jones · Arts & Rec
    Sandra Cestero · Tutoring
    Decision 1 of 5
    Your Choice Analyzed

    Debrief Question for Your Group:

    Simulation Complete!

    Making the Shift: What Changes When You Move from Betterment to Development

    The DVULI workbook presents a clarifying matrix that distinguishes four modes of ministry along two axes: individual versus community, and betterment versus development (DVULI LW2 Workbook, 2024). Individual Betterment (charity) provides skills and activities that help overcome obstacles to self-worth and personal development — it meets immediate needs but does not build lasting capacity. Individual Development fosters capacity and sustainable personal growth and well-being — it is the goal of the Developmental Assets framework. Community Betterment (advocacy) provides resources and activities that mitigate or rectify community problems — it is essential but insufficient. Community Development (transformation) engages residents to build and lead toward positive change and well-being — this is the fullest expression of CYD.

    What does this shift look like in practical terms? It looks like Danny Agosto noticing that a sixteen-year-old gang member had a gift for connecting with younger children on a basketball court and giving him a job as a team coordinator — instead of simply counting him as a participant in a program. It looks like Randall Jones training youth volunteers who are graduates of his arts programs to run classes for neighborhood children — instead of maintaining a professional-to-youth service model permanently. It looks like Sandra Cestero's tutoring program graduates being invited to become tutors themselves — not as a cost-saving measure, but as a deliberate development strategy that honors what they have to give.

    It also looks different in how you measure success. Mishaela Durán reminds us that funders who expect "life change to occur within grant periods" are setting an unrealistic standard for organizations working with young people who have experienced years of accumulated adversity (Durán, 2022). When we shift from betterment to development, our measures of success must shift too: from counting recipients served to tracking the growth of contributors developed; from reporting outputs (programs delivered) to narrating outcomes (leaders emerging); from demonstrating our ministry's competence to documenting the community's expanding capacity.

    Manager Debrief Tip

    If you lead a team of youth workers, this is a critical moment for honest conversation. Ask your team: "Which of our programs actively trains the participants we serve to become program contributors or leaders? Which programs could make that shift in the next six months?" The goal is not to shame anyone for operating at Stage 1 — it is to open a shared imagination for what more is possible. Use the Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework below to turn that conversation into a concrete plan.

    Core Values in the CYD Framework

    One of the deep strengths of CYD as a framework is how naturally it integrates with the five DVULI Core Values. Empowerment — creating "leader-full" organizations by exercising servant leadership and releasing the potential of others — is the Core Value that most directly drives the Developmental Pathway Model. Moving youth from Recipients to Leaders is, at its heart, an empowerment practice. Interdependence — acknowledging and relying on all of God's people and collaborating effectively with the community's talented and committed members — is the Core Value that undergirds ABCD. Every asset map is an act of interdependence made visible (DVULI NC1 Workbook, 2024).

    Leverage — taking small actions that have a powerful and lasting impact — is expressed every time a youth worker identifies a young person's gift and creates a vehicle for it to serve the community. Danny Agosto's mentor Juan Mendoza saw a gift in a sixteen-year-old dealer and gave him a job as a team coordinator. That one act of leverage sent ripples that are still expanding decades later. Accountability — seeking and telling the truth about our own thoughts and actions — is required by the honest self-assessment that CYD demands. Moving from betterment to development requires the courage to acknowledge that our best programs may be, however unintentionally, fostering dependency rather than building capacity. And Balance — maintaining a commitment to wholeness versus busyness — is protected when we shift from trying to do everything for young people to equipping young people to do things themselves. CYD is not just good youth ministry theory; it is a practical expression of everything DVULI has been building toward.

    Stop, Start, Continue, Improve: Your Ministry Reset

    The final movement of this session is the most practical, and perhaps the most important. Everything you have learned across the three sessions of the CYD arc — Developmental Assets, ABCD, and the CYD Developmental Pathway — must now make contact with the specific reality of your ministry. The Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework gives you a structured process for that contact.

    This is not a theoretical exercise. The DVULI LW2 Workbook asks this question directly: "If you could press the reset button on your ministry today, what would you Stop? Start? Continue? Improve?" (DVULI LW2 Workbook, 2024). The answers you generate in this exercise will form the first draft of your Ministry Breakthrough Plan for Session 16 — so bring both honesty and imagination to your work here.

    Stop refers to things that no longer serve the CYD vision — programs or practices that, however well-intentioned, actively reinforce the passive recipient posture, consume resources that could be directed toward development, or communicate to young people that they have nothing to contribute. This category requires the most courage to populate honestly. It is not that "Stop" items are bad — it is that they have become obstacles to something better.

    Start refers to new approaches that the learning of this arc suggests — programs or practices not yet in place that would move your ministry toward the full Developmental Pathway vision. Each "Start" item in the widget below will prompt you to connect it to a specific gap in the Developmental Assets profile you generated in Session 9.

    Continue refers to things already aligned with development rather than betterment — existing programs or practices that are already operating at Stage 2, 3, or 4 of the Developmental Pathway. These deserve to be named and celebrated, and they provide an important foundation for everything the "Start" and "Improve" categories will build on.

    Improve refers to things worth doing better — existing programs that are good but not yet operating at their full developmental potential. Each "Improve" entry in the widget below will prompt you to identify which CYD principle would be most enhanced by the improvement.

    🔄 Stop, Start, Continue, Improve — Ministry Reset Worksheet

    Add ministry activities to each quadrant. For Start items, you'll be prompted to connect them to a Developmental Assets gap. For Improve items, identify the CYD principle most enhanced. Your completed worksheet begins your Ministry Breakthrough Plan.

    🛑 Stop
    Things that no longer serve the CYD vision
    🚀 Start
    New approaches the CYD arc suggests
    ✅ Continue
    Already aligned with development
    🔧 Improve
    Worth doing, but could go further
    Connecting Your Entry

    0 items added across all quadrants
    Your Ministry Reset Summary — Draft 1 of Your Breakthrough Plan
    
      
    
    
    

    
    Your 30-Day Action Plan: One Shift, One Step
    

    
    The final task of this session is deceptively modest but vitally important: identify one specific, concrete action you will take in the next thirty days to shift at least one aspect of your ministry practice from betterment toward development. Not a restructuring of your entire ministry. Not a new five-year strategic plan. One action. One program. One shift in posture.
    

    
    Perhaps it is asking your three most engaged participants in your tutoring program whether they would be willing to be trained as tutors for younger students next month. Perhaps it is inviting the young people in your after-school arts program to help you map the community's assets the way you did with your youth during the Session 10 pre-assignment. Perhaps it is sitting down with Randall Jones's equivalent in your context — the staff member who has the most Stage-3 intuitions — and asking what it would take to give the program graduates somewhere to go next.
    

    
    Sandra Cestero's dilemma was that she had "struggled to find ways for her star graduates to stay involved — there were only so many volunteer jobs in a program that was always cramped for space" (DVULI LW2 Workbook, 2024). The Windsor Court proposal did not solve that problem by adding more volunteer slots to the same program. It solved it by imagining a different kind of program entirely — one in which the whole community became the space, and the young people became the co-designers. Your thirty-day action plan does not need to be Windsor Court. It just needs to be honest, specific, and grounded in what you have learned.
    

    

    
    
      
    Facilitator Delivery Guideline
    
      
    Close the session by having each participant share their 30-day action commitment with a partner from the cohort. Ask them to exchange contact information and agree to a specific check-in date — three to four weeks from now. Accountability for implementation begins before participants leave the room. Emphasize that the Stop, Start, Continue, Improve worksheet they completed in the widget above is the foundation of the Ministry Breakthrough Plan they will develop for Session 16. They should save or screenshot their entries before leaving.
    
    
    

    
    

    
    
      
    Key Takeaways
    
      
      
        
    • Community Youth Development synthesizes Positive Youth Development and Asset-Based Community Development into a single claim: every community develops its youth, and every young person can contribute to community development and transformation.
      • 
        
    • Karen Pittman's foundational insight — "Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged" — demands that youth ministry move beyond betterment toward genuine development of young people as community builders.
      • 
        
    • The Developmental Pathway Model (Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders) provides a concrete progression for rethinking any ministry program. Most ministries operate primarily at Stage 1 and can take identifiable steps toward Stages 2, 3, and 4.
      • 
        
    • The theology of youth discipleship, grounded in Kenda Creasy Dean's research, holds that consequential faith requires creed, community, call, and hope — and that genuine community engagement and leadership is not an optional extension of discipleship but its full expression.
      • 
        
    • DVULI's three goals for a whole-person discipleship ministry — Consequential Faith, Thriving in School/Work/Life, and Community Engagement and Leadership — form an integrated vision that CYD brings to life practically.
      • 
        
    • The Jackson cohort's Windsor Court proposal demonstrates what CYD looks like when applied collaboratively: four ministry leaders, each operating from different institutional contexts, partnering to create a proposal that positions the community's youth as co-designers of neighborhood transformation.
      • 
        
    • The Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework provides a structured entry point for translating CYD learning into specific ministry changes. Your 30-day action plan is the bridge between insight and Breakthrough.
      • 
      
    
    
    

    
    
      
    Looking Ahead
    
      
    Session 12 introduces Scenario Planning — a tool for preparing your ministry for an uncertain future. Having now identified what kind of ministry you want to build (a developmental, CYD-informed ministry that produces consequential faith and community leaders), the next question is: what futures might you face, and how do you prepare strategically for all of them? Bring your Stop, Start, Continue, Improve worksheet and your 30-day action plan notes — they will form the raw material for your ministry Focusing Question in Session 12.
    
    
    

    
    
      
    References
    
      
    Dean, K. C. (2010). Almost Christian: What the faith of our teenagers is telling the American church. New York, NY: Oxford University Press.
    
      
    Durán, M. (2022). Duran: Young people facing challenges need schools and services to work together to support and nurture them as they build their futures. The 74, January 2022.
    
      
    DVULI. (2024). Community youth development: Local workshop 2 pre-assignment. RDV Corporation.
    
      
    DVULI. (2024). Community youth development: Local workshop 2 workbook. RDV Corporation.
    
      
    DVULI. (2024). Core values and healthy change: National conference 1 workbook. RDV Corporation.
    
      
    Jackson Times. (2024, January 20). National program selects Jackson's best [and related articles]. The Jackson Times.
    
      
    Kretzmann, J. P. (1995). Building communities from the inside out. Shelterforce Online, September/October 1995.
    
      
    Lupton, R. D. (1997). Return flight: Community development through reneighboring our cities. Atlanta, GA: FCS Urban Ministries.
    
      
    Raskin, S. (2005). Interview with Lateefah Simon: The transformative power of community youth development. Community Youth Development Journal, Fall 2005.
    
      
    Smith, C., Denton, M. L., Faris, R., & Regnerus, M. (2005). Soul searching: The religious and spiritual lives of American teenagers. New York, NY: Oxford University Press.
    
      
    Travis, T. (2015). Building cathedrals: Urban discipleship that works. Denver, CO: Neighborhood Ministries.

blocks.title: Key Concepts
concepts: Community Youth Development (CYD): every community develops its youth, and every young person can contribute to community development
Developmental Pathway Model: Youth as Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders
Theology of youth discipleship: consequential faith, thriving, and community engagement as the three goals of a whole-person ministry
The Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework for ministry transformation

prompts.prompt: Think back to when YOU were a teenager in church or a youth program. Were you treated more like a project to fix... or a person with something to offer? Be honest.
Name a adult who believed in you before you believed in yourself. What did they actually DO that made the difference? (Bonus: did they know they were doing it?)
prompts.hook: Someone changed the game for you

prompts.prompt: Hot take check: 'Youth ministry is about preparing kids for adulthood' — agree or disagree? What if that framing is actually the problem?
What's something a young person in YOUR community is already doing right now that most adults around them completely overlook or undervalue?
If you had to be honest: in the ministries or programs you've seen, do youth show up as the POINT of the work... or the audience for it? What's the difference?
Finish this sentence with the first thing that comes to mind, then compare with the person next to you: 'A young person is ready to lead when ________.'

activities.title: Pathway Audit: Where Are Your Youth Right Now?
activities.description: Participants examine the Developmental Pathway Model (Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders) by mapping their own ministry's current practices against each stage. First, each participant spends 5 minutes individually on a digital worksheet, placing 3-5 of their real programs or practices onto the four-stage spectrum — being honest about where most of their youth actually land. Then, in breakout groups of 4-5, they compare maps, discuss what assumptions keep youth stuck at the 'Recipients' stage, and identify one concrete shift they could make. Groups return to the main session and drop their 'biggest stuck point' into a shared Padlet wall, which the facilitator screen-shares for a 5-minute whole-group synthesis. The artifact is a completed Pathway Placement Map that participants can bring back to a staff meeting or elder board conversation.
activities.materials: Downloadable Pathway Placement Map worksheet (PDF or fillable form), Padlet board pre-loaded with the four stages, Zoom breakout room assignments
activities.learningGoal: Critically evaluating current ministry design against a developmental framework and identifying concrete structural shifts
activities.scalingNotes: For groups under 15, skip breakout rooms and run the comparison as a full-group gallery walk using screen-share. For groups over 30, assign a designated reporter per breakout group and limit Padlet contributions to one post per group to keep debrief tight.

activities.title: The CYD Flip: Rewriting the Youth Ministry Story
activities.description: This exercise confronts the dominant 'betterment' narrative by having participants literally rewrite it. Each person receives a short, realistic ministry newsletter blurb (provided as a handout) that describes a youth program using deficit language — youth as problems to manage, recipients of adult expertise, future contributors 'someday.' Individually (5 minutes), participants rewrite the blurb through a Community Youth Development lens, centering youth as present-tense contributors to community life. In pairs via breakout room (7 minutes), they read each other's rewrites aloud and mark what changed — language, power dynamics, who is the subject of the sentence. The pair then drafts one guiding sentence they could post above their own desk: 'Every young person in our community is ___.' Pairs paste their sentences into a shared Google Doc visible to the full cohort for a closing 3-minute read-around. The artifact is both the rewritten blurb and the guiding sentence.
activities.materials: Sample deficit-language newsletter blurb (provided as in-app handout), shared Google Doc link, breakout room pairs pre-assigned
activities.learningGoal: Internalizing the CYD paradigm shift by practicing asset-based, community-contribution language in real ministry communications
activities.scalingNotes: For solo self-study users without live cohort access, participants can post their rewritten blurb and guiding sentence to a course discussion board and respond to at least two peers' sentences before moving on. For larger live groups, pairs can be expanded to triads.

activities.title: Three Goals, One Young Person: Whole-Person Case Study
activities.description: Participants apply the theological framework — consequential faith, thriving, and community engagement — to a single realistic youth case study provided in the course materials. The case describes a composite teenager (e.g., a 16-year-old named Marcus who is active in youth group, struggles at home, and feels invisible in his neighborhood) with enough detail to spark genuine ministry discernment. Working individually for 6 minutes, participants complete a Three Goals Analysis Grid, noting where current ministry investment is strong, weak, or absent for someone like Marcus. In breakout groups of 4-5 (10 minutes), participants discuss: 'Which goal do we most easily sacrifice, and why?' Groups surface a single honest answer and bring it to the full room. The facilitator closes with a 5-minute theological reflection connecting the three goals to a whole-person discipleship vision. The artifact is the completed Analysis Grid plus a one-sentence group confession about default ministry blind spots.
activities.materials: Marcus case study handout (PDF or in-app), Three Goals Analysis Grid worksheet, breakout room assignments
activities.learningGoal: Using a theological framework to diagnose ministry wholeness and identify neglected dimensions of youth discipleship
activities.scalingNotes: For asynchronous self-study, participants complete the grid individually and post their 'sacrificed goal' answer to a discussion thread. For cohorts larger than 30, limit full-group debrief to three groups sharing, and use a reaction poll ('Which goal does your ministry most underfund?') to surface patterns quickly.

activities.title: Stop, Start, Continue, Improve: The Ministry Transformation Sprint
activities.description: Participants apply the Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework directly to their own ministry context in a structured, time-boxed sprint. The exercise opens with a 2-minute framing: this is not a wishlist — every 'Start' must be paired with a 'Stop' so the exercise forces real tradeoffs. Participants spend 8 minutes individually filling out a four-quadrant digital worksheet, writing at least two items per quadrant and flagging their highest-leverage item in each. They then move into breakout groups of 4-5, where each person has 90 seconds to pitch their single highest-leverage 'Start' to the group. Group members ask only one question each: 'What would you have to Stop to make room for that?' The full debrief (5 minutes) invites three volunteers to share a Stop-Start pair with the whole cohort, and the facilitator names patterns across the room. The artifact is a completed four-quadrant worksheet — a decision-ready document participants can bring to their next leadership team meeting.
activities.materials: Four-quadrant Stop/Start/Continue/Improve digital worksheet (fillable PDF or Google Form), timer visible to participants, breakout room assignments
activities.learningGoal: Translating the CYD framework into specific, tradeoff-aware ministry decisions using a structured transformation planning tool
activities.scalingNotes: For solo async learners, the worksheet can be completed independently with a follow-up prompt: post your highest-leverage Stop-Start pair in the discussion board and explain the tradeoff you're willing to make. For groups larger than 30, extend breakout time by 3 minutes and have facilitators float between rooms.

activities.title: Community Builder Commissioning: Designing a Real Role
activities.description: This capstone exercise moves from analysis to action: participants design a specific, real role in their ministry or community context where a young person functions as a genuine community builder — not a helper, not a token, but a contributor with real responsibility and real impact. Working individually for 7 minutes, participants complete a Community Builder Role Card, filling in: the young person's name or profile, the community need they are positioned to address, what authority or ownership they will hold, what adult support structures will exist (without undermining youth agency), and how the role reflects consequential faith. In breakout groups of 4-5 (10 minutes), participants pitch their role cards as if presenting to a church board, and peers offer one 'strengthen it' feedback note each. Each participant then revises their card for 2 minutes based on feedback. The final Role Cards are uploaded to a shared course gallery, creating a peer-visible library of real commissioning designs. The artifact is the Role Card itself — actionable enough to implement within 60 days.
activities.materials: Community Builder Role Card template (fillable PDF or in-app form), shared course gallery or discussion board for uploads, breakout room assignments
activities.learningGoal: Synthesizing the full CYD framework and discipleship theology into a concrete, implementable youth leadership role within a real ministry context
activities.scalingNotes: For async self-study, participants post their Role Card to the course gallery and are required to leave one 'strengthen it' comment on a peer's card before the module closes. For cohorts over 30, consider a 'fishbowl' debrief where two or three volunteers share their cards with the full group instead of full-room reporting.

slides.title: From Betterment to Development: Discipling Youth as Community Builders
slides.bodyText: What if the problem with your youth ministry isn't the programming — it's the posture?
slides.speakerNotes: Welcome everyone to Session 11 — the culminating session of our Community Youth Development arc. We've been building toward this moment. Today we're going to name the fundamental shift that changes everything: moving from betterment to development. Before we dive in, ask participants to think for 30 seconds: when they picture a 'successful' youth ministry, what image comes to mind? Hold that image. We're going to challenge it.

slides.title: Two Visions of Youth Ministry
slides.bodyText: Before we can build differently, we have to see the difference between fixing young people and forming them.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition: Most of us entered youth ministry with a vision of helping — and that's good. But there are two very different kinds of helping, and they produce very different kinds of disciples. This section is about naming the gap between betterment and development so we can choose deliberately which one we're doing.

slides.title: The Core Distinction
slides.bodyText: Betterment does things FOR young people. Development does things WITH and through them — because their community needs what they carry.
slides.speakerNotes: Read this slowly. Pause after 'FOR.' Then pause again after 'through them.' Ask the room: 'Which word shows up more often in your ministry's language — helping or equipping? Serving or building?' This is the hinge point of the entire session. Let the silence do the work. Invite one or two reactions before moving on.

slides.title: Betterment vs. Development
bullets: Problem-focused framing
Youth as recipients of care
Success = keeping kids safe
Community stays the same
Ministry serves youth
slides.bodyText: ["Asset-focused framing", "Youth as contributors to community", "Success = unleashing capacity", "Community is transformed", "Youth serve community"]
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each row side by side. Don't rush — let participants sit with each contrast. The most provocative one is 'Youth serve community' versus 'Ministry serves youth.' Ask: which column more accurately describes your current programming? Which column do your job descriptions reflect? Emphasize that neither column is entirely wrong — betterment has its place — but development is the larger frame we've been missing.

slides.title: Community Youth Development's Core Claim
slides.bodyText: "Every community develops its youth — and every young person has the capacity to contribute to the development of their community."
slides.speakerNotes: This is the defining statement of the CYD framework. Unpack both halves: First half — communities are already shaping youth, for better or worse. The question isn't whether formation is happening, but what kind. Second half — this is the radical claim. Not 'some gifted youth' or 'future leaders.' Every. Young. Person. Ask: What does it do to your ministry design if you actually believe this about the teenager who shows up disengaged, the one who's struggling at home, the one nobody else has noticed?

slides.title: The Developmental Pathway Model
slides.bodyText: Youth don't arrive as leaders — but the journey from recipient to leader is a path we can deliberately design.
slides.speakerNotes: Transition into the model. Tell participants: this next section gives us a map. CYD doesn't just give us a philosophy — it gives us a progression. Most ministries are stuck at stage one because they never knew stages two through four existed. Let's walk through all four.

slides.title: The Four Stages: Recipients → Leaders
bullets: Recipients — Youth receive care, programs, and services
Resources — Youth are seen as assets the community can invest in
Assets — Youth contribute skills, energy, and perspective to community
Leaders — Youth drive vision, organize others, and create change
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each stage with a concrete ministry example. Recipients: a food pantry where teens get school supplies. Resources: a tutoring program where teens develop skills. Assets: teens leading a neighborhood cleanup they designed. Leaders: teens organizing a community forum on school safety. Ask: where does most of your programming currently land? Where do you want it to land in three years? Note that all four stages matter — development is about movement through the pathway, not abandoning earlier stages.

slides.title: The Stage Most Ministries Skip
slides.bodyText: We move teenagers from recipients to resources — then stop. The Asset and Leader stages are where discipleship becomes consequential.
slides.speakerNotes: Name this directly. Most youth ministries do stages one and two well — programs, mission trips, service projects. What's missing is sustained engagement where youth are actually driving something that matters in their real community. Ask participants: Can you name one current program that is genuinely at the Asset or Leader stage? If not — that's the invitation.

slides.title: Designing for the Pathway
bullets: Audit where each program falls on the continuum
Create entry points that don't dead-end at 'recipient'
Pair adults as co-laborers, not supervisors
Let teens name real problems in their own community
Celebrate small acts of contribution — not just attendance
slides.speakerNotes: This is practical application. Have participants think about one specific program they run. Walk them through the audit question: Does this program have a next step that moves youth up the pathway? If a teenager thrives in this program, what's the natural progression toward greater agency? The pair-as-co-laborers point is often the hardest for adults who are used to being in charge. Normalize that discomfort — it's a sign of growth.

slides.title: A Theology of Whole-Person Discipleship
slides.bodyText: Youth ministry has often aimed at personal piety. The tradition calls us to something bigger — disciples who are consequential in their world.
slides.speakerNotes: Major transition. We've been talking social science — now we're going theological. Tell participants: the framework we've been building from CYD doesn't contradict Christian formation. It actually recovers something the church has sometimes lost. This section is about grounding the whole conversation in what we believe about who God made young people to be.

slides.title: Kenda Creasy Dean, Almost Christian
slides.bodyText: "The problem with American teenagers is not that they are mean, immoral little beasts. The problem is that they are too nice — and their faith is too small to bother anyone, including themselves."
slides.speakerNotes: Let this land. Read it twice if you need to. Dean's research found that the dominant faith of American teenagers is Moralistic Therapeutic Deism — a tame, personal faith that asks little and changes nothing. Ask: Does that description feel familiar? Then ask the harder question: Is our ministry producing that kind of faith, even unintentionally? The goal of this section is to articulate a richer theological vision — consequential faith that actually costs something and transforms something.

slides.title: Three Goals of Whole-Person Ministry
bullets: Consequential Faith — belief that shapes decisions, risks, and sacrifice
Thriving — flourishing across mind, body, relationships, and vocation
Community Engagement — active participation in the common good
slides.speakerNotes: Walk through each goal carefully. Consequential faith: faith that has costs, not just benefits — faith that changes how a teenager relates to power, money, neighbors, enemies. Thriving: not just emotional wellness, but the full Shalom vision — youth who are becoming whole people. Community engagement: this is where CYD and theology meet. The disciple is not a private believer — she is a neighbor, a builder, a voice. Ask: Which of these three goals shows up most clearly in your current ministry? Which is most absent?

slides.title: Stop, Start, Continue, Improve
bullets: STOP — Programs that keep youth permanently in recipient mode
START — Pathways that move youth toward asset and leader stages
CONTINUE — Relational depth, mentorship, and pastoral care
IMPROVE — Align your language, metrics, and storytelling with development
slides.speakerNotes: This is the ministry transformation framework. Give participants 5-7 minutes to work through this for their own context — individually or in pairs. Then debrief: What was hardest to put in the STOP column? Why? What's one thing you could START in the next 90 days without a budget increase? Emphasize that CONTINUE is important — this framework is not a wrecking ball. Good relational ministry is the foundation everything else builds on. The IMPROVE column is about culture: if you measure attendance but not contribution, you'll keep getting attendance-shaped disciples.

slides.title: The Ministry You're Leaving Behind vs. The Ministry Ahead
bullets: Youth are problems to manage
Success = keeping kids in church
Adults hold all the vision
Community is the backdrop
slides.bodyText: ["Youth are contributors to activate", "Success = disciples who change things", "Adults hold space for youth to lead", "Community is the congregation's mission field"]
slides.speakerNotes: This is the summary contrast. Walk through each row as a 'from/to.' Encourage participants to mark which column they currently live in for each row — it's rarely all one or all the other. The goal isn't guilt; it's clarity. Ask: If you woke up five years from now and your ministry looked like the right column, what would be different about the teenagers you've worked with? What would be different about your neighborhood?

slides.title: The Invitation
slides.bodyText: The church doesn't need better programs for young people. It needs young people who are equipped, sent, and trusted to be the church.
slides.speakerNotes: Deliver this as a commissioning statement, not a critique. Pause after reading it. Ask participants to sit with it for 10 seconds before responding. Then open the floor: What would have to change in your theology, your budget, or your calendar for this to be true in your ministry? Close the session with prayer — specifically naming youth by name who are already contributing to their communities, even in small ways. End with the recognition that this work is holy.

slides.title: Key Takeaways
bullets: CYD holds two truths together: every community shapes youth, and every youth can shape community
The Developmental Pathway moves youth from recipients to leaders — by design, not accident
Whole-person discipleship aims at consequential faith, thriving, and community engagement
Stop, Start, Continue, Improve gives you a practical tool for honest ministry audit
The shift from betterment to development is ultimately a theological one — it's about who we believe young people are
slides.speakerNotes: Read through these slowly as a closing summary. Invite participants to circle the one takeaway that challenged them most. Offer 2 minutes of quiet reflection, then a closing pair-share: 'What is one thing you want to carry out of this room and into your ministry this week?' Close with gratitude for the hard, holy work of youth ministry — and remind them that the teenagers in their care are not the church of tomorrow. They are the church of today.

blocks.title: Audiobook
blocks.transcript:
Imagine a youth pastor — call her Maria — who has led an excellent after-school tutoring program for six years. Every week, thirty neighborhood teenagers show up to receive homework help from volunteer tutors. Attendance is consistent. Parents are grateful. The church board is proud. By every traditional measure, it is a successful ministry. But Maria has begun to feel an uncomfortable weight. The students file in, receive help, file out, and return the following week in exactly the same posture: needing help, receiving help, leaving. The best students graduate and move on. The program continues as it always has. Something is missing, though Maria cannot yet name it.

What Maria is beginning to sense is the difference between BETTERMENT and DEVELOPMENT — the very distinction that Robert Lupton described as the line between programs that improve conditions for people and programs that build the capacity of communities to transform themselves from within. In this session, we name that difference clearly, we equip you to act on it, and we anchor the whole enterprise in the most compelling reason of all: this is precisely what it means to disciple young people as followers of Jesus Christ.

[short pause]

This session is the third and final movement in DVULI's Community Youth Development arc. In Session nine, you were introduced to the forty Developmental Assets — the Search Institute's research-based framework identifying the internal and external building blocks that young people need to grow up healthy, resilient, and purposeful. You assessed three young people from your ministry context and discovered, likely with some sobering clarity, which assets were most and least present in their lives. You also encountered Kenda Creasy Dean's concept of CONSEQUENTIAL FAITH — the four characteristics that distinguish highly devoted young people from their peers who hold only a nominal, or moralistic therapeutic, form of Christianity. That work comes from her book Almost Christian, published in 2010.

In Session ten, you shifted focus from the individual to the neighborhood. Through John Kretzmann's framework known as Asset-Based Community Development, you learned to read your community not through its deficits but through its gifts — the skills of individual residents, the power of citizens' associations, and the resources of local institutions. As Kretzmann described in 1995, you began to see the neighborhood differently: not as a problem to be solved, but as a place where God is already at work.

Session eleven now asks: what happens when you put these two streams together? What emerges when positive youth development meets asset-based community development? The answer is Community Youth Development — and its central claim is both simple and revolutionary.

[short pause]

Community Youth Development, as articulated by Karen Pittman and the Forum for Youth Investment, rests on two parallel claims that must be held together. The first is that every community contributes to the development of its young people. Young people do not develop in isolation from the neighborhoods, congregations, schools, and relationships that surround them. Whether those environments are nurturing or harmful, they are never neutral. The forty Developmental Assets framework gives us a vocabulary for naming what those contributions look like when they are working well. The Asset-Based Community Development framework gives us a methodology for strengthening those contributions by mobilizing what is already present.

The second claim is the one most youth ministries have not yet fully inhabited: every young person can offer gifts and experiences in the process of community development and transformation. This claim is not optimistic sentiment — it is a theological and empirical conviction. Theologically, it flows from the biblical truth that every person is created in the image of God and has been given gifts for the building up of the Body, as Paul writes in First Corinthians chapter twelve, verse seven, and as Peter echoes in First Peter chapter four, verses ten and eleven. Empirically, it is confirmed in the story of Lateefah Simon, who moved from unenthusiastic participant in a youth development program to executive director — and then to MacArthur Foundation Fellow — because someone saw her gifts before she could see them herself. That story was documented by Raskin in 2005 in the Community Youth Development Journal.

[short pause]

Consider this phrase from Karen Pittman carefully: Problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged. This is not merely a motivational slogan. It is a complete critique of two common approaches to youth ministry. The first approach — betterment — aims to make young people problem-free by addressing their deficits. It is, at its best, compassionate and necessary. But it stops too soon. The second approach — a more sophisticated version of betterment — aims to prepare young people with skills, knowledge, and faith. This is better, but it still stops short of the full vision. The third approach — development in the fullest sense — engages young people as active contributors to community transformation. It is this third approach that Community Youth Development demands of us.

[short pause]

The DVULI curriculum presents a four-stage model for describing how youth can progressively move from passive recipients of ministry services toward becoming active leaders of community transformation. This DEVELOPMENTAL PATHWAY MODEL is not a ladder of prestige — it is a map of growing participation and contribution. Most youth ministries, even excellent ones, operate primarily at Stage one. The challenge of this session is to help you envision and plan for progression through all four stages.

At Stage one, youth are Recipients. The ministry mobilizes resources to provide services for youth. Programs are designed by adults, delivered by adults, and received by young people who are positioned as beneficiaries. This is not inherently wrong — it is where all ministry must begin. The tutoring program that brings students off dangerous streets and into a caring space is doing something real and important. The danger is when Stage one becomes the permanent ceiling of our ambition for young people, and when we unconsciously communicate that they have nothing to contribute beyond their presence as recipients of our care.

At Stage two, youth are Resources. The ministry equips and mobilizes youth to support others. The student who was once tutored becomes a tutor. The teenager who was mentored begins mentoring a younger child. The young person who participated in a drama program becomes a drama leader. Danny Agosto's story is a powerful illustration of this stage. Juan Mendoza, seeing Agosto's gift for connecting with younger children on the basketball court, gave him a job as a team coordinator — and in that moment, an at-risk gang member began the journey that would eventually lead him to a Master's degree in Theology and a Latino Leadership Award.

At Stage three, youth are Assets. The ministry mobilizes youth to contribute to community betterment. Young people are no longer just helping within a ministry program — they are contributing to the wider community as agents of positive change. This is the stage at which the forty Developmental Assets of individual young people begin to express themselves as community assets. A teenager with strong service-to-others assets does not just volunteer — she organizes. A young person with strong positive-identity assets does not just participate in a neighborhood cleanup — he leads one. Stage three represents the organic connection between individual development and community development that Community Youth Development envisions.

At Stage four, youth are Leaders. The ministry is developing youth who grow into adults equipped to transform their community. This is not just leadership development within the ministry program — it is the cultivation of the next generation of community leaders who will carry forward a vision of community transformation long after they have aged out of any particular youth program. Lateefah Simon's trajectory — from petty thief to MacArthur Fellow to civic leader — represents Stage four in its fullest expression, as documented by Raskin in 2005.

[short pause]

Before moving forward, take a moment to think about one specific program or activity in your ministry. Which stage of the Developmental Pathway best describes how youth currently participate in that program? And what would it concretely look like for that program to shift to the next stage? Keep that program in mind as we continue — we will return to it.

[short pause]

Why does the shift from betterment to development matter theologically? The answer begins with an honest reckoning with the state of youth faith formation in American churches. Since 2001, sociologist Christian Smith and his colleagues at Notre Dame have been studying the spiritual lives of American adolescents. Their findings are summarized in landmark works including Soul Searching from 2005 and Almost Christian, which Kenda Creasy Dean published in 2010. The findings are sobering for everyone in youth ministry.

Smith found that while most American teenagers hold religious beliefs, those beliefs are surprisingly shallow and inarticulate. The dominant theology he identified — what he called MORALISTIC THERAPEUTIC DEISM — is characterized by a distant, undemanding God who is mainly concerned that everyone feel good and be nice to one another. One seventeen-year-old African-American Protestant girl summarized it this way, as quoted in the DVULI workbook: "I guess I'm a Christian, but I'm one of those still trying to figure everything out. I believe there's a higher power, but that's about all I know for sure." This is not the faith of disciples who follow Jesus into the suffering of the world. It is the faith of a morally comfortable self — and organized religion is largely producing it.

[short pause]

What would a genuinely consequential faith look like? Drawing on Smith's data to identify what distinguished the most highly devoted teenagers from their peers, Dean described four consistent characteristics.

The first is a creed to believe — the ability to articulate beliefs about a God who is both personal and powerful. These young people do not hold a vague theism; they have a vocabulary for what they believe and why.

The second is a community to belong to — including meaningful relationships with adults who speak honestly with them about faith and life. Not the insulated peer-only gatherings so common in youth group culture, but intergenerational relationships of genuine accountability and care.

The third is a call to live out — lives oriented by a sense of divine vocation to serve others, rather than the pursuit of self-satisfaction. These young people do not experience faith as a restraint on their ambitions; they experience it as a summons toward a purpose larger than themselves.

The fourth is a hope to hold onto — a sense that their lives are part of a larger story that is going somewhere, guided by God. This hope is not naive optimism; it is the eschatological confidence that the arc of history bends toward the Kingdom.

[short pause]

Now hold these four characteristics alongside the Developmental Pathway Model, and something remarkable comes into focus. A young person operating as a Recipient in your ministry is unlikely to develop a strong sense of call — because they have never been invited to contribute. A young person who has never been mobilized as a community Asset will struggle to maintain a robust hope — because they have never experienced the transformative joy of seeing their gifts change something in the world. The developmental pathway is not merely a framework for program design; it is a pathway toward consecrated personhood.

[short pause]

DVULI's approach to youth discipleship integrates the Community Youth Development framework with Dean's theology of consequential faith into three interdependent goals that together describe what whole-person ministry looks like. Understanding these three goals as a unified vision — not a checklist — is essential for ministry leaders who want to move beyond betterment toward genuine development.

The first goal is Consequential Faith. This means intentionally building all four of Dean's characteristics into every dimension of ministry: creed — teaching that takes doctrine seriously and gives youth a vocabulary for their beliefs; community — creating genuine intergenerational relationships, not just peer gatherings; call — inviting young people into meaningful service and leadership; and hope — narrating their lives within God's grand story of redemption and restoration. The warning is real: despite strong levels of spiritual activity during teen years, sixty-one percent of today's young adults disengage from active faith by their twenties, according to Barna Research cited in the DVULI workbook. Consequential faith is the antidote.

The second goal is Thriving in School, Work, and Life. Discipleship that does not engage the whole person — including their academic and vocational development — is incomplete. Sandra Cestero put it plainly: instead of focusing on spiritual values and character building, many programs revolve around tutoring and teaching kids basic skills like reading, and even those on track to graduate high school have difficulty with something as fundamental as filling out a job application. Effective youth development ministry attends to the concrete realities of thriving in the world — not as a substitute for spiritual formation, but as an expression of it. The forty Developmental Assets framework, especially its commitment-to-learning and social-competencies categories, provides a concrete vocabulary for this goal.

The third goal is Community Engagement and Leadership. It is not enough to form young people who are spiritually mature and personally capable. The goal is to develop young people who are actively engaged in the transformation of the communities where they live. This is not an optional extension of discipleship for the especially motivated — it is the natural expression of what it means to follow Jesus who, as Ted Travis wrote in 2015, sees every young person not as a rock to be managed but as a cathedral to be built. Community engagement and leadership is the full-fledged expression of Stage four in the Developmental Pathway.

[short pause]

Abstract frameworks become transformative only when they are tested in concrete situations. The Jackson DVULI cohort's response to the Windsor Court Community Center controversy gives us exactly that opportunity. A developer, backed by three new upscale residents and city planning officials, proposed to convert an abandoned community center into office and retail space — a coffee shop, a law firm, a frozen yogurt shop, and an insurance branch. For long-term residents of a neighborhood that was forty-three percent African-American and forty-seven percent Latino, the writing on the wall was clear: gentrification was arriving, and the community's most valuable gathering space was about to become inaccessible to them.

Four DVULI participants decided they would respond — not with protest alone, but with a proposal. Understanding both the power of asset-based community development and the potential of Community Youth Development, they set out to imagine an alternative use for the space that would mobilize the community's youth as genuine partners in neighborhood transformation.

Reverend Leroy Burnett, Senior Pastor at Leonard Avenue Community Church, saw the Windsor Court situation as a way to reconnect his aging congregation with the neighborhood and to give the church's dwindling youth program something more exciting and purposeful than it had ever offered. Danny Agosto, Youth Pastor at Iglesia Pentecostal Unida, saw the proposal as a natural extension of his mentoring initiative — community service and community leadership fitting perfectly with his strategy of replacing the gang's false promises of belonging and purpose with genuine alternatives. Randall Jones, Recreation Coordinator at the Urban Center for Success, had already developed arts and recreation programs that young people loved, and he knew several graduates who were ready to become program leaders. And Sandra Cestero, Youth Leader and Program Coordinator at Grace Christian Family Center, had run a successful tutoring and basic skills program for years and had begun to struggle with the recurring problem of her star graduates having nowhere to go next. She was hungry for a vehicle that would allow those graduates to stay involved as contributors rather than departing as alumni.

What united these four leaders was precisely what Community Youth Development demands: each of them had already moved, in their own ministries, at least partway along the Developmental Pathway. Each of them was already thinking about youth not just as recipients of services but as resources with gifts. The Windsor Court proposal gave them an opportunity to imagine, together, what Stage three and Stage four could look like at a neighborhood scale.

[short pause]

The DVULI workbook presents a clarifying framework that distinguishes four modes of ministry along two axes: individual versus community, and betterment versus development. Individual Betterment — what we might call charity — provides skills and activities that help overcome obstacles to self-worth and personal development. It meets immediate needs but does not build lasting capacity. Individual Development fosters capacity and sustainable personal growth and well-being — it is the goal of the Developmental Assets framework. Community Betterment, or advocacy, provides resources and activities that mitigate or rectify community problems — it is essential but insufficient. And Community Development, which is transformation in the fullest sense, engages residents to build and lead toward positive change and well-being. This is the fullest expression of Community Youth Development.

What does this shift look like in practical terms? It looks like Danny Agosto noticing that a sixteen-year-old gang member had a gift for connecting with younger children on a basketball court and giving him a job as a team coordinator — instead of simply counting him as a participant in a program. It looks like Randall Jones training youth volunteers who are graduates of his arts programs to run classes for neighborhood children — instead of maintaining a professional-to-youth service model permanently. It looks like Sandra Cestero's tutoring program graduates being invited to become tutors themselves — not as a cost-saving measure, but as a deliberate development strategy that honors what they have to give.

It also looks different in how you measure success. As Mishaela Durán reminded us in 2022, funders who expect life change to occur within grant periods are setting an unrealistic standard for organizations working with young people who have experienced years of accumulated adversity. When we shift from betterment to development, our measures of success must shift too: from counting recipients served to tracking the growth of contributors developed; from reporting outputs — programs delivered — to narrating outcomes — leaders emerging; from demonstrating our ministry's competence to documenting the community's expanding capacity.

[short pause]

One of the deep strengths of Community Youth Development as a framework is how naturally it integrates with the five DVULI Core Values. EMPOWERMENT — creating leader-full organizations by exercising servant leadership and releasing the potential of others — is the Core Value that most directly drives the Developmental Pathway Model. Moving youth from Recipients to Leaders is, at its heart, an empowerment practice. Interdependence — acknowledging and relying on all of God's people and collaborating effectively with the community's talented and committed members — is the Core Value that undergirds asset-based community development. Every asset map is an act of interdependence made visible.

Leverage — taking small actions that have a powerful and lasting impact — is expressed every time a youth worker identifies a young person's gift and creates a vehicle for it to serve the community. Danny Agosto's mentor Juan Mendoza saw a gift in a sixteen-year-old and gave him a job as a team coordinator. That one act of leverage sent ripples that are still expanding decades later. Accountability — seeking and telling the truth about our own thoughts and actions — is required by the honest self-assessment that Community Youth Development demands. Moving from betterment to development requires the courage to acknowledge that our best programs may be, however unintentionally, fostering dependency rather than building capacity. And Balance — maintaining a commitment to wholeness versus busyness — is protected when we shift from trying to do everything for young people to equipping young people to do things themselves. Community Youth Development is not just good youth ministry theory; it is a practical expression of everything DVULI has been building toward.

[short pause]
