The final movement of this session is the most practical, and perhaps the most important. Everything you have learned across the three sessions of the Community Youth Development arc — Developmental Assets, asset-based community development, and the Developmental Pathway — must now make contact with the specific reality of your ministry. The Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework gives you a structured process for that contact.

This is not a theoretical exercise. The DVULI workbook asks this question directly: if you could press the reset button on your ministry today, what would you Stop? Start? Continue? Improve? The answers you generate will form the first draft of your Ministry Breakthrough Plan — so bring both honesty and imagination to your work here.

Stop refers to things that no longer serve the Community Youth Development vision — programs or practices that, however well-intentioned, actively reinforce the passive recipient posture, consume resources that could be directed toward development, or communicate to young people that they have nothing to contribute. This category requires the most courage to populate honestly. It is not that Stop items are bad — it is that they have become obstacles to something better.

Start refers to new approaches that the learning of this arc suggests — programs or practices not yet in place that would move your ministry toward the full Developmental Pathway vision. As you identify each Start item, connect it to a specific gap in the Developmental Assets profile you generated in Session nine.

Continue refers to things already aligned with development rather than betterment — existing programs or practices already operating at Stage two, three, or four of the Developmental Pathway. These deserve to be named and celebrated, and they provide an important foundation for everything the Start and Improve categories will build on.

Improve refers to things worth doing better — existing programs that are good but not yet operating at their full developmental potential. For each Improve entry, identify which Community Youth Development principle would be most enhanced by the improvement.

[short pause]

The final task of this session is deceptively modest but vitally important: identify one specific, concrete action you will take in the next thirty days to shift at least one aspect of your ministry practice from betterment toward development. Not a restructuring of your entire ministry. Not a new five-year strategic plan. One action. One program. One shift in posture.

Perhaps it is asking your three most engaged participants in your tutoring program whether they would be willing to be trained as tutors for younger students next month. Perhaps it is inviting the young people in your after-school arts program to help you map the community's assets the way you did during Session ten. Perhaps it is sitting down with the staff member who has the most Stage-three intuitions on your team and asking what it would take to give the program graduates somewhere to go next.

Sandra Cestero's dilemma was that she had struggled to find ways for her star graduates to stay involved — there were only so many volunteer jobs in a program that was always cramped for space. The Windsor Court proposal did not solve that problem by adding more volunteer slots to the same program. It solved it by imagining a different kind of program entirely — one in which the whole community became the space, and the young people became the co-designers. Your thirty-day action plan does not need to be Windsor Court. It just needs to be honest, specific, and grounded in what you have learned.

[short pause]

Let's bring together the key ideas from this session. Community Youth Development synthesizes positive youth development and asset-based community development into a single claim: every community develops its youth, and every young person can contribute to community development and transformation. Karen Pittman's foundational insight — problem-free is not fully-prepared, and fully-prepared is not fully-engaged — demands that youth ministry move beyond betterment toward genuine development of young people as community builders.

The Developmental Pathway Model, moving from Recipients to Resources to Assets to Leaders, provides a concrete progression for rethinking any ministry program. Most ministries operate primarily at Stage one and can take identifiable steps toward Stages two, three, and four. The theology of youth discipleship, grounded in Kenda Creasy Dean's research, holds that consequential faith requires creed, community, call, and hope — and that genuine community engagement and leadership is not an optional extension of discipleship but its full expression.

DVULI's three goals for a whole-person discipleship ministry — Consequential Faith, Thriving in School, Work, and Life, and Community Engagement and Leadership — form an integrated vision that Community Youth Development brings to life practically. And the Jackson cohort's Windsor Court proposal demonstrates what Community Youth Development looks like when applied collaboratively: four ministry leaders, each operating from different institutional contexts, partnering to create a proposal that positions the community's youth as co-designers of neighborhood transformation.

The Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework provides a structured entry point for translating this learning into specific ministry changes. Your thirty-day action plan is the bridge between insight and breakthrough.

[short pause]

Looking ahead, Session twelve introduces Scenario Planning — a tool for preparing your ministry for an uncertain future. Having now identified what kind of ministry you want to build — a developmental, Community Youth Development-informed ministry that produces consequential faith and community leaders — the next question is: what futures might you face, and how do you prepare strategically for all of them? Bring your Stop, Start, Continue, Improve reflections and your thirty-day action plan notes — they will form the raw material for your ministry focusing question in Session twelve.

blocks.title: Infographic
blocks.prompt:
Create a polished educational infographic in 16:9 landscape format with a light cream background (#faf8f5). Title at the top in bold dark navy (#00558C) text: "The Developmental Pathway: From Betterment to Development". Subtitle in smaller italic gray text: "Discipling Youth as Community Builders".

The centerpiece is a large horizontal pathway flowing left to right across the middle of the infographic, composed of four connected arrow-shaped chevron stages, each progressively larger to show growth. Stage 1 is labeled "RECIPIENTS" in a muted gray-blue box with a small icon of an open hand receiving, and the sub-label "Youth receive services". Stage 2 is labeled "RESOURCES" in a lighter teal box with an icon of a puzzle piece, sub-label "Youth have strengths to tap". Stage 3 is labeled "ASSETS" in a green (#319B41) box with an icon of a rising bar chart, sub-label "Youth add measurable value". Stage 4 is labeled "LEADERS" in a bold deep navy (#00558C) box with an icon of a person with a star, sub-label "Youth drive community change". A thin dotted arrow runs beneath the entire pathway labeled "BETTERMENT approach" on the left end and "DEVELOPMENT approach" on the right end, showing the paradigm shift.

Above the pathway, place a banner reading "Core Principle" with the text: "Every community develops its youth — every young person can contribute to community development" in warm amber (#d97706) accent styling.

Below the pathway, display three circular icons in a row representing the three goals of whole-person youth discipleship. Circle 1 is navy (#00558C) with a flame icon labeled "Consequential Faith". Circle 2 is green (#319B41) with a sprouting plant icon labeled "Thriving". Circle 3 is warm amber (#d97706) with interconnected people icons labeled "Community Engagement". A thin connecting line links all three circles with the header above them: "Three Goals of Whole-Person Discipleship".

In the bottom-right corner, place a small compact 2x2 grid titled "Ministry Transformation Framework" with four quadrant cells labeled "STOP" in red, "START" in green (#319B41), "CONTINUE" in navy (#00558C), and "IMPROVE" in amber (#d97706), each with a simple corresponding icon (X, plus sign, checkmark, upward arrow).

Use clean sans-serif typography throughout, generous white space, subtle drop shadows on cards and chevrons, and thin connecting lines with small directional arrows to show relationships between sections. The overall aesthetic should be modern, minimal, and professional — like a seminary course visual aid.

config.concept: Developmental Pathway Model: Youth as Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders
config.description: An interactive four-stage pathway widget displaying the Recipients → Resources → Assets → Leaders progression as a horizontal road with four milestones. Participants are asked to think about one specific program or activity in their ministry and drag a progress marker to the stage that currently describes their approach to youth in that program. The widget then generates four 'upgrade questions' — one for each stage beyond the current one — asking what would need to change for youth to progress to the next stage. For example, 'What would it look like for the youth in your tutoring program to become tutors themselves?' A side panel shows case study examples from the Jackson DVULI cohort demonstrating each stage.
config.rationale: Mapping a real program on the pathway and receiving specific upgrade questions for each subsequent stage transforms an abstract developmental continuum into an actionable planning tool — participants leave with concrete questions to bring back to their ministry team.

config.concept: CYD in action: applying all three community development frameworks to a real case
config.description: An interactive scenario simulator based on the Jackson Times Windsor Court Community Center case. Participants are introduced to the four DVULI Jackson participants (Burnett, Agosto, Jones, Cestero) and their ministry contexts, then presented with a series of five decisions the coalition must make about the Windsor Court proposal (Which programs to include? How to involve youth as partners? Which community stakeholders to engage first? How to respond to gentrification concerns? How to measure success differently from the original developer's metrics?). For each decision, three options are presented; participants vote, and the widget reveals how their choices align with ABCD principles, CYD principles, and the five Core Values — generating a debrief discussion prompt.
config.rationale: The decision simulator uses the Jackson case — characters participants have encountered in pre-reading — as a familiar-yet-complex scenario that requires integrating multiple frameworks simultaneously, modeling the kind of messy, interleaved thinking that real ministry leadership demands.

blocks.title: Stop, Start, Continue, Improve Ministry Reset

config.concept: Stop, Start, Continue, Improve framework for ministry transformation
config.description: A four-quadrant interactive planning worksheet where participants enter ministry activities into each of four categories: Stop (things that no longer serve the CYD vision), Start (new approaches the session's learning suggests), Continue (things already aligned with development rather than betterment), and Improve (things worth doing better). For each entry in 'Start,' a prompt links the new activity to a specific deficit in the Developmental Assets profile from Session 9. For each entry in 'Improve,' a prompt asks which CYD principle would be most enhanced by the improvement. The completed worksheet becomes the first draft of the participant's Ministry Breakthrough planning for Session 16.
config.rationale: The explicit link between 'Start' entries and Session 9's asset-deficit data creates a spaced retrieval moment — participants must recall and apply specific asset categories from two sessions ago to justify new ministry initiatives, deepening both the asset framework and the planning framework simultaneously.

videos.title: Community Catalysts Nashville's Holistic Vision for Youth
videos.rationale: This video highlights a community-centered approach to youth development, emphasizing youth thriving and their role as leaders and contributors, directly aligning with the chapter's focus on youth as community builders and fostering thriving.
